1: Paul could not address them as pneumatics (spiritual people) but as fleshly, as infants in Christ. The growth-metaphor is Christological: Christian maturity means increasing conformity to Christ crucified — deepening understanding of the cross as God's wisdom, increasing willingness to bear weakness as Christ bore it. The Corinthians' factional behavior shows they have not advanced in this Christological formation.
2: Milk, not solid food: elementary Christological teaching vs. the deeper dimensions of the cross and resurrection. The progression from milk to solid food in Paul's context is not from the cross to something beyond it but from surface acquaintance with the cross to full understanding of its cosmic scope — its victory over the powers, its creation of a new humanity, its eschatological implications in the resurrection of 1 Corinthians 15:12-58.
3: Jealousy and quarreling as evidence of fleshly (sarkikos) existence: conformity to the pattern of this age rather than to the crucified Christ. The cross is the model for the community's social ethics: Christ did not compete for status or assert privilege but gave himself. A community shaped by the cross cannot be characterized by competitive factionalism without contradicting its own foundation.
4: Following Paul or following Apollos: the factional allegiances reveal a this-age, uncrucified social logic. To say I follow Paul is to evaluate Paul as a patron-figure rather than as a servant of the crucified Christ. The test of genuine Christological formation is whether the community's social life reflects the servant-pattern of Christ or the status-competition pattern of the surrounding culture.
5: Paul and Apollos are servants through whom you came to believe, as the Lord assigned. The word diakonoi (servants/ministers) intentionally echoes Christ's own self-description: the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve (Mark 10:45). Apostolic ministry is Christ-shaped servanthood, not patron-client leadership. The Lord who assigns their roles is the criterion by which they are evaluated.
6: Paul planted, Apollos watered, God gave the growth: the community belongs to no apostle but to God, who accomplishes growth through Christ. The founding and watering of the community are ultimately Christ's acts through human instruments — the same Christ who is the foundation (1 Corinthians 3:11), the community's life-source, and the one in whom all things are held together (Colossians 1:17).
7: Neither the planter nor the waterer is anything — only God who gives the growth. This radical relativization of apostolic importance is grounded in Christology: if Christ is the foundation and the Spirit is the life-source, then apostles are merely conduits. The community's ultimate loyalty is to Christ, whose growth-giving activity makes all apostolic work possible.
8: Each will be rewarded according to their own labor: the eschatological evaluation of apostolic work. The reward is given by Christ on the Day of the Lord (1 Corinthians 3:13: the Day will reveal it), not by popular acclaim. The quality of community-building work is measured by its conformity to the cross-shaped pattern of Christ's own ministry — servant-orientation, not prestige-seeking.
9: You are God's field, God's building: the community belongs to God, not to any apostolic patron. The building metaphor (oikodomē) will develop into the temple metaphor (naos, 1 Corinthians 3:16), where the community is the dwelling-place of God's Spirit — constituted by Christ's redemption and the Spirit's indwelling. The Christological foundation makes the building God's property.
10: By the grace God gave me, Paul laid a foundation as a wise builder: Paul's apostolic work is grace-given, not self-achieved. The foundation he laid is Christ himself (1 Corinthians 3:11) — not a body of doctrine about Christ, but Christ in person as the community's ground. The grace enabling Paul's ministry is the same grace that makes Christ the foundation: both are undeserved divine acts.
11: No one can lay a foundation other than the one already laid, which is Jesus Christ: the most direct Christological claim in 1 Corinthians 3. Christ himself is the foundation — not a teaching about Christ, not an apostolic tradition, but the person of Jesus Christ as the community's ontological ground. This is the decisive refutation of apostolic factionalism: the foundation is not Paul, not Apollos, not Peter, but Christ alone.
12: Building on this foundation with gold, silver, stones, wood, hay, straw: different qualities of community-building work. The quality is measured by how closely it conforms to the Christ-shaped pattern of the foundation — servant-oriented, cross-bearing, Spirit-dependent. The eschatological fire will reveal which community-building work was genuinely Christological and which was sub-Christological performance.
13: The Day will reveal the quality of each work by fire: the Day of the Lord (Christ's return) is the eschatological quality-test. This Christological judgment is not about personal salvation but apostolic stewardship: how faithfully did each builder build the community on the Christ-foundation? The fire of Christ's judgment purifies and reveals what is lasting (genuine Christological formation) from what is temporary (mere social performance).
14: If what was built survives, the builder will receive a reward: eschatological commendation from Christ for faithful Christological community-building. The reward (misthos) is given by the one who is both foundation and judge — Christ himself. The apostle's ultimate accountability is not to the community that evaluates their rhetoric but to Christ who evaluates the quality of their building work.
15: If burned up, the builder will be saved but as through fire — suffering loss of reward but not of salvation. Christological security (being saved) is not conditional on apostolic performance quality, but reward is. This establishes Christ as the guarantor of the community's ultimate standing even when the building work has been substandard — salvation rests on the foundation, not the superstructure.
16: You are the temple of God and the Spirit of God dwells in you: the community as Christ's new temple. In the OT, YHWH's presence dwelt in the Tabernacle and Temple; in the NT, that presence has relocated to the community constituted by Christ's redemption. The Spirit who dwells in the community is the Spirit of Christ (Romans 8:9) — the community is made a temple by Christ's atoning work and the Spirit's indwelling.
17: Destroying God's temple brings divine destruction: the holiness of the community is Christ-grounded and therefore non-negotiable. The factional behavior, the immorality, the competitive wisdom-displays are all forms of temple-destruction — attacks on the community that Christ purchased and the Spirit indwells. The severity of the warning reflects the seriousness of the Christological violation: you are destroying what Christ died to create.
18: Do not deceive yourselves — if you think yourself wise by this age's standards, become a fool in order to become wise. The cross demands a foolishness-crossing from this-age wisdom to the wisdom of God. The entry into genuine wisdom is through the apparent foolishness of the cross: acknowledging that Christ crucified is wiser than all human systems. Becoming a fool means accepting the cross's judgment on human wisdom-projects.
19: The wisdom of this world is foolishness in God's sight: the cross-reversal applies to epistemology as much as to social status. God catches the wise in their craftiness (Job 5:13): the very sophistication that enables them to construct elaborate wisdom-systems becomes the trap. Christ crucified exposes the pretensions of all wisdom that does not reckon with the cross.
20: The Lord knows that the thoughts of the wise are futile (Psalms 94:11): divine omniscience renders all human wisdom-projects ultimately empty before the cross. The contrast is between human scheming (dialogismous) and God's actual purposes revealed in Christ. The cross is the place where human wisdom-projects go to die and God's purposes are vindicated.
21: No more boasting about human leaders — all things are yours: the anti-boasting conclusion leads to an extraordinary counter-claim. Because Christ is the foundation and the Lord of all apostles, those who belong to Christ possess all apostles, not just one faction's favorite. The Christological inversion: client-mentality (I follow Paul) impoverishes; Christ-belonging enriches beyond measure.
22: Paul, Apollos, Cephas, the world, life, death, present, future — all yours: the eschatological scope of Christological belonging. In Christ, believers already own the entire created order — not in the sense of present possession but in the sense of ultimate inheritance (Romans 8:17). The present age and all its powers, including death itself, belong to those who belong to Christ (Romans 8:38-39). The factions competing over apostles are like children fighting over crumbs when they already own the bakery.
23: You are of Christ, and Christ is of God: the relational chain that grounds everything. The community's belonging to Christ is the same in kind (though different in nature) as Christ's belonging to the Father. As the Son relates to the Father in eternal generation and love, so believers relate to Christ in grace-union and participation. This Trinitarian chain is the deepest anti-faction argument: you belong to the one who belongs to God — your identity is secured at that level, not at the level of apostolic patronage.
